title: Measuring Strategy-Decay Risk: Minimum Regime Performance and the Durability of Systematic Investing
summary: This paper introduces a framework called Minimum Regime Performance (MRP) to assess the durability of systematic investment strategies across changing market regimes. MRP is defined as the lowest realized risk-adjusted return across distinct historical regimes, serving as a lower bound on strategy robustness. The authors apply this measure to a broad universe of established factor strategies and find a consistent trade-off between efficiency and resilience, where strategies with higher long-term Sharpe ratios do not always exhibit higher MRPs.
key_points: Introduces Minimum Regime Performance (MRP) as a quantitative framework for assessing systematic strategy durability
MRP is defined as the lowest realized risk-adjusted return across distinct historical market regimes
The measure captures how performance deteriorates when underlying relationships weaken or competitive pressures compress alpha
Applied to a broad universe of established factor strategies
Findings reveal a trade-off between efficiency and resilience: high Sharpe ratio strategies do not always have high MRPs
The framework aims to complement traditional measures of market and liquidity risk with a diagnostic for strategy-decay risk
why_it_matters: This paper provides quantitative practitioners and portfolio managers with a new risk metric for evaluating systematic strategies. Understanding MRP helps investors identify strategies that may appear robust on average but contain hidden fragility when regimes shift. While focused on investment strategy risk rather than market structure or macro catalysts, the framework offers a novel diagnostic for identifying strategy-decay risk that complements traditional measures of market and liquidity risk.
